name: Creole
description: Louisiana Creole cuisine blends French, Spanish, West African, Caribbean, and Indigenous influences. It favors aromatic vegetable bases, tomatoes in many stews/sauces, seafood-forward dishes, layered seasoning, and rice-centered meals. Compared to Cajun, Creole cooking is often more city-influenced, frequently uses tomatoes, and leans into refined sauces while still being hearty and bold.

signatureBases.name: Holy Trinity
signatureBases.notes: Core aromatic base for many Creole dishes; often softened in butter/oil before adding spices, stock, or tomatoes.

signatureBases.name: Trinity + Garlic
signatureBases.notes: Common enhancement; garlic is frequently added after the trinity softens.

signatureBases.name: Creole Tomato Base
signatureBases.notes: Many Creole stews and sauces build body with tomato paste and acidity with tomatoes.

techniques.name: Sweating aromatics
techniques.description: Cook trinity in butter/oil until softened and fragrant; avoid browning for cleaner sauces.

techniques.name: Roux (light to medium)
techniques.description: Creole roux often stays lighter than Cajun gumbo roux, supporting sauces and etouffees without heavy roast notes.

techniques.name: Tomato-building
techniques.description: Use tomato paste briefly cooked in fat to deepen flavor, then add tomatoes/stock for sauces.

techniques.name: Seafood timing
techniques.description: Add seafood near the end to avoid toughness; keep simmer gentle.

techniques.name: Rice-forward plating
techniques.description: Many stews and sauces are served over rice; rice texture matters (separate grains, not mushy).

glutenFree.notes: Many dishes can be GF with a cornstarch slurry instead of roux, or GF flour blends; avoid wheat-based roux unless adapted.

dairyFree.notes: Use oil instead of butter where needed; many dishes are naturally dairy-free.

lowCarb.notes: Serve stews over cauliflower rice; reduce sugar/tomato additions if needed.
